sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 13, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Arrow keys move ALL shapes (black F, dark gray F, yellow L) together in the corresponding direction. Each move shifts by ~3 rows/cols. SPACE toggles the top-right corner cell. The yellow strip on the right edge appears to be a moves-remaining counter (consumed top-to-bottom).
- Goal model: Need to figure out the target configuration. The black F has 5 white dots that may indicate a target pattern or position.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 32 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Very interesting! Now I can see what's happening. The black F has moved right and is now overlapping with the separator and the dark gray F. Looking at the area:
Row 15: 'bbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbb' - black F top bar
Row 16: 'bbBWBBSBBWBbbbbbbbbbb' - black F with white dots, separator (SSS) in middle
Row 17: 'bbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbb' - black F top bar
Row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb' - dark gray F (ccc) on left, separator (SSS), black F (BBB) on right
Row 19: 'bbcccSSSBWBbbbbbbbbbb' - dark gray F, separator, black F with white dot
...
So the black F and dark gray F are now overlapping/interacting. The dark gray F's vertical bar (ccc) is now to the LEFT of the separator, and the black F's vertical bar is to the RIGHT of the separator. They seem to have "merged" or "combined" in some way.
Looking at the segmentation:
- Node 5: Black F (40 pixels) at cols 27-35
- Node 9: Dark gray (18 pixels) at cols 27-29, rows 18-23 - this is just the vertical bar part of the dark gray F!
- The dark gray F's top bar seems to have been absorbed/merged with the black F
Wait, let me reconsider. The dark gray F originally had 45 pixels. Now node 9 has 18 pixels (just the vertical bar). The black F still has 40 pixels. So the dark gray F's top bar (which would be about 27 pixels: 9 wide × 3 high) seems to have been absorbed.
Actually, looking more carefully at the segmentation:
- Node 5: B, 40 pixels - the black F
- Node 9: c, 18 pixels - this is the dark gray vertical bar (6 rows × 3 cols = 18)
- The dark gray F's top bar is missing!
This suggests that when the black F overlaps with the dark gray F, the overlapping parts merge or one absorbs the other. The dark gray F's top bar has been "consumed" by the black F.
This might be the key mechanic! The goal could be to move the black F to overlap with and "consume" the dark gray F, and then possibly the yellow L as well.
Let me check: the dark gray F originally had 45 pixels. Its shape is:
- Top bar: 9 wide × 3 high = 27 pixels
- Middle bar: 5 wide × 1 high = 5 pixels (but this might overlap with the top bar)
- Vertical bar: 3 wide × 6 high = 18 pixels
Actually, the F shape has:
- Top horizontal bar: rows 15-17, cols 36-44 (9×3 = 27 pixels)
- Middle horizontal bar: rows 17-18, cols 36-38 (but wait, the boundary shows [17, 44], [17, 39], [18, 38])
- Vertical bar: rows 18-23, cols 36-38 (6×3 = 18 pixels)
Hmm, let me reconsider. The original dark gray F boundary was:
[[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44], [17, 39], [18, 38], [23, 38], [23, 36]]
This traces:
- (15, 36) to (15, 44) - top edge
- (15, 44) to (17, 44) - right edge of top bar
- (17, 44) to (17, 39) - bottom edge of top bar (from right to left, stopping at col 39)
- (17, 39) to (18, 38) - diagonal step down-left
- (18, 38) to (23, 38) - right edge of vertical bar
- (23, 38) to (23, 36) - bottom edge of vertical bar
- (23, 36) back to (15, 36) - left edge
So the shape is:
- Top bar: rows 15-17, cols 36-44 (9 wide × 3 high)
- Middle bar: row 17, cols 36-38 (part of the top bar's left side, extending down)
- Vertical bar: rows 18-23, cols 36-38 (3 wide × 6 high)
Total: 27 + 18 = 45 pixels (but there might be some overlap)
Now, after the RIGHT moves, the dark gray F's top bar has been absorbed, leaving only the vertical bar (18 pixels). This suggests that the black F "consumed" the overlapping part of the dark gray F.
The goal might be to completely consume the dark gray F and the yellow L by moving the black F to overlap with them.
But wait - the black F and dark gray F moved together when I pressed RIGHT. So they maintain their relative positions. The black F is at cols 27-35 and the dark gray F's remaining part is at cols 27-29. They're overlapping!
Actually, I think what happened is that the black F moved right, and when it overlapped with the separator, the dark gray F got "split" - the part that overlapped with the black F was consumed, leaving only the non-overlapping part.
Let me think about this more carefully. The original positions were:
- Black F: cols 18-26
- Separator: cols 28-30 (SSS)
- Dark gray F: cols 36-44
After 3 RIGHT moves (each moving 3 cols):
- Black F: cols 27-35
- Separator: cols 28-30 (still there, since it's part of the background)
- Dark gray F: would be at cols 45-53, but...
Wait, but the dark gray F's remaining part is at cols 27-29, which is to the LEFT of the separator. This doesn't make sense if all shapes move together.
Let me re-examine. Maybe the dark gray F didn't move right with the black F. Maybe only the black F moves, and the dark gray F and yellow L are stationary targets!
Let me check this hypothesis. Originally:
- Black F: cols 18-26
- Dark gray F: cols 36-44
After 3 RIGHT moves:
- Black F: cols 27-35 (moved right by 9)
- Dark gray F's remaining part: cols 27-29
If the dark gray F stayed stationary, it would still be at cols 36-44. But the remaining part is at cols 27-29, which is 9 columns to the left of the original position. This suggests the dark gray F DID move right by 9 columns, but then... something happened.
Actually wait, let me re-read the segmentation. Node 9 has boundary [[18, 27], [18, 29], [23, 29], [23, 27]], which is cols 27-29, rows 18-23. This is a 3×6 rectangle.
If the dark gray F originally was at cols 36-44 and moved right by 9, it would be at cols 45-53. But the remaining part is at cols 27-29. This doesn't match.
Unless... the dark gray F didn't move at all! Let me check: the original dark gray F was at cols 36-44. The remaining part is at cols 27-29. If the dark gray F stayed stationary, its vertical bar would be at cols 36-38, not 27-29.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me reconsider the movement. Maybe the shapes don't all move together. Maybe only the black F moves, and the other shapes are stationary.
Let me check the original positions vs current positions:
- Original black F: cols 18-26
- Current black F: cols 27-35
- Movement: +9 cols (3 RIGHT × 3)
- Original dark gray F: cols 36-44
- Current dark gray F remaining: cols 27-29
- If stationary: should be at cols 36-44
- If moved +9: should be at cols 45-53
Neither matches cols 27-29. So something else is going on.
Wait, maybe I need to reconsider. The dark gray F's original boundary was:
[[15, 36], [15, 44], [17, 44], [17, 39], [18, 38], [23, 38], [23, 36]]
The vertical bar part is at cols 36-38 (from the boundary points [18, 38] to [23, 38] and [23, 36]).
After the RIGHT moves, the remaining dark gray part is at cols 27-29. If the dark gray F moved right by 9, the vertical bar would be at cols 45-47. But it's at cols 27-29.
Unless the dark gray F moved LEFT by 9? That would put the vertical bar at cols 27-29. But why would it move left when I pressed RIGHT?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the movement mechanics. Let me look at the LEFT action result again:
- Black F moved left by 1 (from cols 18-26 to cols 17-25)
- Dark gray F also seemed to move... but I'm not sure.
Let me check the initial state more carefully. In the very first frame (before any actions), the black F was at cols 18-26 and the dark gray F was at cols 36-44.
After SPACE (first action), the shapes didn't move (only the top-right corner changed).
After LEFT (second action), the black F moved left. Did the dark gray F move too?
Looking at the LEFT result:
Row 15 before: 'bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBbbbSSSbbbcccccccccbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbY'
Row 15 after:  'bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbSSSbbbbbbcccccccccbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbY'
The black F (BBBBBBBBB) moved from cols 18-26 to cols 17-25 (left by 1).
The dark gray F (ccccccccc) was at cols... let me count:
Before: bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(17 b's)BBBBBBBBB(9 B's)bbb(3 b's)SSS(3 S's)bbb(3 b's)ccccccccc(9 c's)bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(18 b's)Y
After:  bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(15 b's)BBBBBBBBB(9 B's)bbbbbb(6 b's)SSS(3 S's)bbbbbb(6 b's)ccccccccc(9 c's)bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(15 b's)Y
Wait, the dark gray F (ccccccccc) seems to have stayed at the same position in both cases. Let me count more carefully...
Before LEFT:
- 17 b's, 9 B's, 3 b's, 3 S's, 3 b's, 9 c's, 18 b's, 1 Y = 63 chars (plus newline)
After LEFT:
- 15 b's, 9 B's, 6 b's, 3 S's, 6 b's, 9 c's, 15 b's, 1 Y = 63 chars
The black F moved left by 2 (from 17 b's to 15 b's before it). But the dark gray F stayed at the same position (still 9 c's after the same number of b's from the separator).
Wait, that's not right either. Let me count from the separator (SSS):
Before: SSSbbbccccccccc (3 b's between SSS and c)
After:  SSSbbbbbbccccccccc (6 b's between SSS and c)
So the dark gray F moved RIGHT by 3 after I pressed LEFT! That's the opposite direction from the black F.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me reconsider: maybe the black F and dark gray F move in OPPOSITE directions? When I press LEFT, the black F moves left and the dark gray F moves right. When I press RIGHT, the black F moves right and the dark gray F moves left.
Let me verify this hypothesis:
- Original dark gray F: cols 36-44
- After LEFT: dark gray F should be at cols 39-47 (moved right by 3)
- After 3 RIGHT: dark gray F should be at cols 30-38 (moved left by 9)
The current dark gray F remaining part is at cols 27-29. If the dark gray F moved left by 9 from cols 36-44, it would be at cols 27-35. The vertical bar (originally cols 36-38) would be at cols 27-29. That matches!
So the hypothesis is: when I press RIGHT, the black F moves right AND the dark gray F moves left (they move toward each other). When I press LEFT, the black F moves left AND the dark gray F moves right (they move away from each other).
This makes sense! The goal is probably to move the black F and dark gray F so they overlap, and the overlapping parts "merge" or "consume" each other. The white dots might indicate which parts should overlap.
Now, looking at the current state:
- Black F: cols 27-35
- Dark gray F remaining: cols 27-29 (vertical bar only)
- The dark gray F's top bar has been consumed/merged
The dark gray F's vertical bar is still visible at cols 27-29. If I move RIGHT again, the black F would move further right and the dark gray F would move further left, potentially consuming more of the dark gray F.
But wait, the dark gray F's vertical bar is at cols 27-29, and the black F's vertical bar is at cols 33-35. They're not overlapping yet. Let me think about what happens when they do overlap.
Actually, looking at the current ascii:
Row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
Row 19: 'bbcccSSSBWBbbbbbbbbbb'
The dark gray vertical bar (ccc) is at cols 27-29, and the black F's vertical bar (BBB/BWB) is at cols 33-35. They're separated by the separator (SSS) at cols 30-32.
If I press RIGHT again:
- Black F moves right by 3: cols 30-38
- Dark gray F moves left by 3: cols 24-26
- They would be even further apart!
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me reconsider the movement direction.
Actually, I think I had it backwards. Let me re-examine:
- After LEFT, the black F moved left (cols 18-26 → 17-25)
- After LEFT, the dark gray F moved... let me check the b's between SSS and c:
  Before: SSSbbbccccccccc (3 b's)
  After:  SSSbbbbbbccccccccc (6 b's)
So the dark gray F moved RIGHT (away from the separator) by 3 columns. This means LEFT moves the black F left and the dark gray F right (away from each other).
Conversely, RIGHT should move the black F right and the dark gray F left (toward each other).
After 3 RIGHT moves:
- Black F: cols 18-26 → 27-35 (moved right by 9)
- Dark gray F: cols 36-44 → 27-35 (moved left by 9)
So both shapes are now at cols 27-35! They're overlapping! The dark gray F's top bar has been consumed/merged with the black F's top bar, leaving only the dark gray vertical bar at cols 27-29.
Wait, but the black F is also at cols 27-35. So the dark gray vertical bar at cols 27-29 is overlapping with the left part of the black F.
Hmm, but looking at the ascii:
Row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
The ccc is at cols 27-29, SSS is at cols 30-32, and BBB is at cols 33-35. So they're NOT overlapping - they're separated by the separator.
But if both shapes moved to cols 27-35, they should be at the same position. Unless the separator prevents them from overlapping...
Actually, I think the separator (SSS) acts as a barrier. The black F is on the left side of the separator, and the dark gray F is on the right side. When they move toward each other, they meet at the separator but can't cross it.
But wait, the black F is at cols 27-35, which includes cols 33-35 on the right side of the separator (cols 30-32). So the black F has crossed the separator!
Let me re-examine the ascii:
Row 15: 'bbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
Row 16: 'bbBWBBSBBWBbbbbbbbbbb'
The black F's top bar is at cols 27-35 (9 B's). But looking at row 16, there's 'BWBBSBBWB' which includes the separator (SSS) at cols 30-32. So the black F is overlapping with the separator.
And row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
The ccc (dark gray) is at cols 27-29, SSS (separator) is at cols 30-32, and BBB (black) is at cols 33-35.
So the black F's vertical bar is at cols 33-35 (right of separator), and the dark gray F's vertical bar is at cols 27-29 (left of separator). They're on opposite sides of the separator but at the same vertical position.
This suggests that when the shapes overlap, they "split" at the separator, with each shape's part ending up on its original side of the separator. The black F's top bar (which was originally on the left) is now partially on the right side of the separator, and the dark gray F's top bar (which was originally on the right) has been consumed/merged.
I think the key insight is that the goal is to completely merge the black F with the dark gray F by moving them to overlap. The white dots might indicate the "correct" overlap pattern.
Let me try pressing RIGHT one more time to see what happens when the shapes overlap more.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: Arrow keys move the black F and dark gray F in OPPOSITE horizontal directions (toward/away from each other). Vertical moves affect all shapes together. When shapes overlap, parts get consumed/merged. The separator (SSS) seems to act as a boundary.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: black F at cols 27-35, dark gray F vertical bar at cols 27-29
# They're partially overlapping. Let me try RIGHT to see if more merging happens
# Or maybe I should try LEFT to separate them and understand the mechanic better

# Let me first check: what if I press RIGHT again?
# Black F would move right, dark gray F would move left (more overlap)
# But the dark gray F is already mostly consumed...

# Let me try RIGHT to see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 100:
        print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the area around separator
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Area around separator ===")
for i in range(14, 25):
    print(f"Row {i}: '{lines[i][25:46]}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

So the shape is:

Actually, I think what happened is that the black F moved right, and when it overlapped with the separator, the dark gray F got "split" - the part that overlapped with the black F was consumed, leaving only the non-overlapping part.

- Dark gray F: cols 36-44

If the dark gray F stayed stationary, it would still be at cols 36-44. But the remaining part is at cols 27-29, which is 9 columns to the left of the original position. This suggests the dark gray F DID move right by 9 columns, but then... something happened.
Actually wait, let me re-read the segmentation. Node 9 has boundary [[18, 27], [18, 29], [23, 29], [23, 27]], which is cols 27-29, rows 18-23. This is a 3×6 rectangle.
If the dark gray F originally was at cols 36-44 and moved right by 9, it would be at cols 45-53. But the remaining part is at cols 27-29. This doesn't match.
Unless... the dark gray F didn't move at all! Let me check: the original dark gray F was at cols 36-44. The remaining part is at cols 27-29. If the dark gray F stayed stationary, its vertical bar would be at cols 36-38, not 27-29.
Hmm, this is confusing. Let me reconsider the movement. Maybe the shapes don't all move together. Maybe only the black F moves, and the other shapes are stationary.
Let me check the original positions vs current positions:

Neither matches cols 27-29. So something else is going on.

Unless the dark gray F moved LEFT by 9? That would put the vertical bar at cols 27-29. But why would it move left when I pressed RIGHT?
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the movement mechanics. Let me look at the LEFT action result again:

- Dark gray F also seemed to move... but I'm not sure.
Let me check the initial state more carefully. In the very first frame (before any actions), the black F was at cols 18-26 and the dark gray F was at cols 36-44.
After SPACE (first action), the shapes didn't move (only the top-right corner changed).

Row 15 after:  'bbbbbbbbbbbbbbbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbSSSbbbbbbcccccccccbbbbbbbbbbbbbbbY'

After:  bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(15 b's)BBBBBBBBB(9 B's)bbbbbb(6 b's)SSS(3 S's)bbbbbb(6 b's)ccccccccc(9 c's)bbbbbbbbbbbbbbb(15 b's)Y
Wait, the dark gray F (ccccccccc) seems to have stayed at the same position in both cases. Let me count more carefully...

The black F moved left by 2 (from 17 b's to 15 b's before it). But the dark gray F stayed at the same position (still 9 c's after the same number of b's from the separator).
Wait, that's not right either. Let me count from the separator (SSS):

So the dark gray F moved RIGHT by 3 after I pressed LEFT! That's the opposite direction from the black F.
Hmm, this is getting complicated. Let me reconsider: maybe the black F and dark gray F move in OPPOSITE directions? When I press LEFT, the black F moves left and the dark gray F moves right. When I press RIGHT, the black F moves right and the dark gray F moves left.

So the hypothesis is: when I press RIGHT, the black F moves right AND the dark gray F moves left (they move toward each other). When I press LEFT, the black F moves left AND the dark gray F moves right (they move away from each other).
This makes sense! The goal is probably to move the black F and dark gray F so they overlap, and the overlapping parts "merge" or "consume" each other. The white dots might indicate which parts should overlap.
Now, looking at the current state:

The dark gray F's vertical bar is still visible at cols 27-29. If I move RIGHT again, the black F would move further right and the dark gray F would move further left, potentially consuming more of the dark gray F.

Actually, I think I had it backwards. Let me re-examine:

So the dark gray F moved RIGHT (away from the separator) by 3 columns. This means LEFT moves the black F left and the dark gray F right (away from each other).
Conversely, RIGHT should move the black F right and the dark gray F left (toward each other).
After 3 RIGHT moves:
- Black F: cols 18-26 → 27-35 (moved right by 9)
- Dark gray F: cols 36-44 → 27-35 (moved left by 9)
So both shapes are now at cols 27-35! They're overlapping! The dark gray F's top bar has been consumed/merged with the black F's top bar, leaving only the dark gray vertical bar at cols 27-29.
Wait, but the black F is also at cols 27-35. So the dark gray vertical bar at cols 27-29 is overlapping with the left part of the black F.
Hmm, but looking at the ascii:
Row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
The ccc is at cols 27-29, SSS is at cols 30-32, and BBB is at cols 33-35. So they're NOT overlapping - they're separated by the separator.
But if both shapes moved to cols 27-35, they should be at the same position. Unless the separator prevents them from overlapping...
Actually, I think the separator (SSS) acts as a barrier. The black F is on the left side of the separator, and the dark gray F is on the right side. When they move toward each other, they meet at the separator but can't cross it.
But wait, the black F is at cols 27-35, which includes cols 33-35 on the right side of the separator (cols 30-32). So the black F has crossed the separator!
Let me re-examine the ascii:
Row 15: 'bbBBBBBBBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
Row 16: 'bbBWBBSBBWBbbbbbbbbbb'
The black F's top bar is at cols 27-35 (9 B's). But looking at row 16, there's 'BWBBSBBWB' which includes the separator (SSS) at cols 30-32. So the black F is overlapping with the separator.
And row 18: 'bbcccSSSBBBbbbbbbbbbb'
The ccc (dark gray) is at cols 27-29, SSS (separator) is at cols 30-32, and BBB (black) is at cols 33-35.
So the black F's vertical bar is at cols 33-35 (right of separator), and the dark gray F's vertical bar is at cols 27-29 (left of separator). They're on opposite sides of the separator but at the same vertical position.
This suggests that when the shapes overlap, they "split" at the separator, with each shape's part ending up on its original side of the separator. The black F's top bar (which was originally on the left) is now partially on the right side of the separator, and the dark gray F's top bar (which was originally on the right) has been consumed/merged.
I think the key insight is that the goal is to completely merge the black F with the dark gray F by moving them to overlap. The white dots might indicate the "correct" overlap pattern.
Let me try pressing RIGHT one more time to see what happens when the shapes overlap more.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model updated: Arrow keys move the black F and dark gray F in OPPOSITE horizontal directions (toward/away from each other). Vertical moves affect all shapes together. When shapes overlap, parts get consumed/merged. The separator (SSS) seems to act as a boundary.

<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current state: black F at cols 27-35, dark gray F vertical bar at cols 27-29
# They're partially overlapping. Let me try RIGHT to see if more merging happens

# But the dark gray F is already mostly consumed...

# Let me try RIGHT to see what happens
result = action(['RIGHT'])
print(f"RIGHT result: board_changed={result.get('board_changed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 100:
        print(f"Node {n['id']}: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check the area around separator
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\n=== Area around separator ===")
for i in range(14, 25):
    print(f"Row {i}: '{lines[i][25:46]}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
